test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to eMedNY manuals for pharmacy claim guidance, while earlier policy text says dual eligible members are billed through Medicare rather than Medicaid for immunization services. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy chunk does not define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing in detail; it says, "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Earlier policy text states, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and also that "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says, "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025], and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as central to dual eligible billing: policy says dual eligible members "will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says pharmacy providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay first for dual eligible claims: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and pays only after other reimbursement sources are exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], while the provider manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at the Medicaid-only amount and says no additional payment is made if Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: Policy is less specific and mostly directs providers to the manuals for pharmacy claim guidance: "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. The provider manual gives the operative billing rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including the payment cap and Medicare assignment requirement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Earlier policy language says, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], which is narrower and framed around immunization services, whereas the provider manual addresses dual eligible pharmacy claims generally and requires Medicare enrollment/claim submission for Medicare-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as central to dual eligible billing: policy says dual eligible members "will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says pharmacy providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay first for dual eligible claims: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and pays only after other reimbursement sources are exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], while the provider manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at the Medicaid-only amount and says no additional payment is made if Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Policy is less specific and mostly directs providers to the manuals for pharmacy claim guidance: "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. The provider manual gives the operative billing rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including the payment cap and Medicare assignment requirement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Earlier policy language says, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], which is narrower and framed around immunization services, whereas the provider manual addresses dual eligible pharmacy claims generally and requires Medicare enrollment/claim submission for Medicare-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The newest policy chunk available here does not itself define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing; it points to the provider manual for guidance [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. The provider manual is the more recent controlling source for this issue based on the provided dates [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
5.0 Utilization Management Programs
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_2.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to eMedNY manuals and billing guidance rather than stating the detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule itself. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence does not define a detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule; it says practitioners should refer to the eMedNY provider manual for additional program policy and billing guidance: "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] Older policy guidance states that "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] It also states that "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made"; it also says "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] For dual eligible members, "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" and pharmacies must have the needed Medicare enrollment when required for items billed to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy claims and require Medicare-related billing before Medicaid payment is considered: "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and Medicaid is "the payer of last resort" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted duplicate payment: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and must be "accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts are less detailed and, in the newest policy source, defer providers to the eMedNY manuals for "additional program policy and billing guidance," whereas the provider manual gives the operative payment and claim rules directly. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]

Older policy language specifically says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that dual eligible members "will continue to access immunization services through Medicare," but the provider manual excerpt provided here does not repeat that immunization-specific limitation; instead, it focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for Medicare-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy claims and require Medicare-related billing before Medicaid payment is considered: "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and Medicaid is "the payer of last resort" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted duplicate payment: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and must be "accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are less detailed and, in the newest policy source, defer providers to the eMedNY manuals for "additional program policy and billing guidance," whereas the provider manual gives the operative payment and claim rules directly. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]

Older policy language specifically says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that dual eligible members "will continue to access immunization services through Medicare," but the provider manual excerpt provided here does not repeat that immunization-specific limitation; instead, it focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for Medicare-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The newest policy excerpt is a referral statement rather than a full dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule, so the provider manual should guide current operational billing questions. The policy excerpts include older background guidance that remains relevant, but the 2025 provider manual is the more current source for this issue. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to eMedNY manuals and billing guidance rather than stating the detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule itself. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence does not define a detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule; it says practitioners should refer to the eMedNY provider manual for additional program policy and billing guidance: \"Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance.\" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] Older policy guidance states that \"Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted\" and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] It also states that \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made\"; it also says \"The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] For dual eligible members, \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" and pharmacies must have the needed Medicare enrollment when required for items billed to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy claims and require Medicare-related billing before Medicaid payment is considered: \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" and Medicaid is \"the payer of last resort\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says pharmacies must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted duplicate payment: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment \"will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and must be \"accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are less detailed and, in the newest policy source, defer providers to the eMedNY manuals for \"additional program policy and billing guidance,\" whereas the provider manual gives the operative payment and claim rules directly. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Older policy language specifically says \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" and that dual eligible members \"will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,\" but the provider manual excerpt provided here does not repeat that immunization-specific limitation; instead, it focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for Medicare-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The newest policy excerpt is a referral statement rather than a full dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule, so the provider manual should guide current operational billing questions. The policy excerpts include older background guidance that remains relevant, but the 2025 provider manual is the more current source for this issue. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]"}
test_id_2.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_2.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_2.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_3.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-12-01 and is consistent in direction, but it is older than the provider manual guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says pharmacies “will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while “Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] The later policy update also directs providers to refer pharmacy claims to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guidance and ProDUR/ECCA manual rather than restating a separate dual-eligible billing rule. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that “the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient,” and if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, “no additional payment will be made.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers “are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members,” and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources recognize that dual eligible pharmacy claims involve Medicare as part of the billing/payment framework rather than Medicaid paying as if the member were Medicaid-only. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources point providers away from direct Medicaid-only billing for dual eligible situations and toward Medicare-related handling. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpt is narrower and says pharmacies “will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” with dual eligible members accessing immunization services through Medicare, while the provider manual addresses broader dual-eligible pharmacy claim payment and Medicare enrollment/assignment requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives a specific payment cap rule—Medicare plus Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and no additional payment is made if Medicare pays more—whereas the policy excerpts provided do not state that payment-cap formula. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
comparison: Both sources recognize that dual eligible pharmacy claims involve Medicare as part of the billing/payment framework rather than Medicaid paying as if the member were Medicaid-only. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources point providers away from direct Medicaid-only billing for dual eligible situations and toward Medicare-related handling. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt is narrower and says pharmacies “will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” with dual eligible members accessing immunization services through Medicare, while the provider manual addresses broader dual-eligible pharmacy claim payment and Medicare enrollment/assignment requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives a specific payment cap rule—Medicare plus Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and no additional payment is made if Medicare pays more—whereas the policy excerpts provided do not state that payment-cap formula. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the cited pharmacy/non-dual-eligible and immunization-related statements; the April 2023 and December 2024 policy excerpts mainly point readers to other billing guidance rather than restating the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

5.0 Utilization Management Programs
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member.

test_id_4.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and points providers to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guidelines and provider manual for pharmacy claims guidance, while the provider manual gives the current dual-eligible billing/payment rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says that for pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guidelines and provider manual: "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and "Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. The older policy guidance specific to dual eligible members states: "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. The newest policy source also says: "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says: "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" and that Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy-related claims/services: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the provider manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources direct providers away from treating Medicaid as the first payer for dual eligible pharmacy claims and instead require use of Medicare-related billing/payment rules: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted, while the provider manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at the Medicaid-only amount and requires acceptance as payment in full [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts are more general and mainly point providers to other billing guidance, stating "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to" the eMedNY pharmacy billing guidelines and ProDUR/ECCA manual, rather than laying out the full dual-eligible payment rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023].

The provider manual gives the more specific current operational rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy-related claims/services: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the provider manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources direct providers away from treating Medicaid as the first payer for dual eligible pharmacy claims and instead require use of Medicare-related billing/payment rules: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted, while the provider manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at the Medicaid-only amount and requires acceptance as payment in full [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are more general and mainly point providers to other billing guidance, stating "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to" the eMedNY pharmacy billing guidelines and ProDUR/ECCA manual, rather than laying out the full dual-eligible payment rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023].

The provider manual gives the more specific current operational rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not include a newer policy passage that directly states the current dual-eligible pharmacy claim payment rule; the newest policy source instead refers readers to the provider manual for additional guidance [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_4.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_4.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_4.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_5.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is older and mainly points providers back to the pharmacy billing guidelines and related manuals rather than restating the dual-eligible claim rule in detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence does not define a detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule; it says, "Pharmacy claims For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and also points to the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. Earlier policy guidance states, "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made"; it also says "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers "are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment when Medicare is involved: policy directs providers to billing guidance rather than creating a separate Medicaid-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that no extra payment is made if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts are indirect and referral-based for pharmacy claims, saying providers should "refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and to the ProDUR/ECCA manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual gives the operative payment rule for Medicare/Medicaid coordination [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more specific: it expressly requires Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers to "submit claims and accept assignments" for Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpts only say Medicare must be billed first and Medicaid pays after other reimbursement is exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment when Medicare is involved: policy directs providers to billing guidance rather than creating a separate Medicaid-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that no extra payment is made if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are indirect and referral-based for pharmacy claims, saying providers should "refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and to the ProDUR/ECCA manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual gives the operative payment rule for Medicare/Medicaid coordination [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more specific: it expressly requires Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers to "submit claims and accept assignments" for Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpts only say Medicare must be billed first and Medicaid pays after other reimbursement is exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a full current dual-eligible pharmacy claim definition; they mainly reference other billing manuals and older general coordination-of-benefits language. The provider manual is the more recent and more specific source for current guidance on this issue [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
