Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The billing guidelines also place responsibility on the provider to determine other coverage and whether it covers the service billed: \"It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: \"providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and \"a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources state Medicaid is not the primary payer when other responsible coverage exists: \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and \"If there is a responsible third party who should be paying... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more explicit about primary payer responsibility, stating that \"a responsible third party... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The provided provider manual excerpts do not include a dual-eligible-specific pharmacy claim rule beyond the general primary-payor/TPL language, so the dual-eligible application is inferred only to the extent the text expressly addresses Medicare and other third-party liability. The newest provider manual source is later than the newest policy source, so it should guide current interpretation unless a historical date is intended."}
test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

test_id_10.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy source dated 2025-09-01 says Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that Medicare must be billed first, while the provider manual adds that Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update September 2025 Volume 41 Number 9:2-3 — Sep 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that for dual eligible enrollees, "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The newer policy update also says the manual update includes "emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules," but it does not further define co-payment responsibility in the provided text. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual says that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states, "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from" the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be primary before Medicaid for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual limits combined Medicare/Medicaid payment and addresses Medicare-covered services for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat dual-eligible members as subject to Medicare-related rules: policy says the July 2025 update includes "emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025], and the provider manual states that Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts focus on billing order and payer hierarchy, stating only that Medicaid is last resort and Medicare must be billed first; they do not expressly address whether a provider may recover co-pay, deductible, or coinsurance from the member in the provided text. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific on member cost-sharing responsibility, stating that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from" the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require Medicare to be primary before Medicaid for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual limits combined Medicare/Medicaid payment and addresses Medicare-covered services for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat dual-eligible members as subject to Medicare-related rules: policy says the July 2025 update includes "emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025], and the provider manual states that Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts focus on billing order and payer hierarchy, stating only that Medicaid is last resort and Medicare must be billed first; they do not expressly address whether a provider may recover co-pay, deductible, or coinsurance from the member in the provided text. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific on member cost-sharing responsibility, stating that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from" the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct co-payment rule for dual-eligible members, so the policy-side comparison is limited to payer order and Medicare-first billing language. The provider manual excerpt is from pharmacy policy guidelines and specifically mentions Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance; it does not provide a broader dual-eligible co-payment rule for all services in the provided text. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2025 Volume 41 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The newer policy update also says the manual update includes "emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules," but it does not provide a specific rule on co-payment responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update September 2025 Volume 41 Number 9:2-3 — Sep 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says that for dual-eligible members, "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made." It also states, "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says, "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" and that Medicaid only pays for certain excluded drugs for dual-eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary and Medicaid as secondary for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first, while the manual says the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources limit Medicaid billing for dual-eligible members to situations where Medicare does not fully cover the service or drug: policy requires third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare to be billed before Medicaid, and the manual says Medicaid only pays for certain drugs excluded from Part D coverage. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpts do not specifically assign co-payment responsibility; they only state that Medicaid pays after other reimbursement sources are exhausted and that Medicare must be billed first. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update September 2025 Volume 41 Number 9:2-3 — Sep 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific on payment responsibility, stating that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a pharmacy provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as primary and Medicaid as secondary for dual-eligible members: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first, while the manual says the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources limit Medicaid billing for dual-eligible members to situations where Medicare does not fully cover the service or drug: policy requires third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare to be billed before Medicaid, and the manual says Medicaid only pays for certain drugs excluded from Part D coverage. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts do not specifically assign co-payment responsibility; they only state that Medicaid pays after other reimbursement sources are exhausted and that Medicare must be billed first. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update September 2025 Volume 41 Number 9:2-3 — Sep 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific on payment responsibility, stating that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a pharmacy provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond payer-of-last-resort and Medicare-first billing rules. The provider manual excerpt is pharmacy-specific and references Medicare Part B and Part D, so its co-payment language should be read in that pharmacy context only. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:

to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

test_id_12.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_12.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_12.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2024-05-01 and 2025-07-01 say providers cannot attempt to recover co-payment/coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals and must accept Medicare/Medicaid payment as payment in full, while the provider manual adds the same payment-in-full rule and specifically addresses Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance recovery.
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" and "[t]he provider is required to accept the Medicare ..." payment terms as stated in the update [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. Earlier policy guidance also states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. The July 2025 policy update notes that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" but does not add a more specific co-payment rule in the provided excerpt [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — 2025-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "[i]f the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made"; it also states that "[t]he Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It further says that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources prohibit shifting Medicare-related cost-sharing to the dual-eligible member and require acceptance of the program payment as full payment: policy says providers "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], and the provider manual says payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reflect that Medicaid is secondary to Medicare for dual-eligible billing: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the provider manual limits total Medicare/Medicaid payment and addresses Medicare cost-sharing recovery [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about the type of cost-sharing at issue, stating that a pharmacy provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt speaks more generally about "any co-payment or co-insurance amount" for "Medicare Part C" benefit providers [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].

The provider manual includes a payment cap rule—"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "no additional payment will be made" if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]—which is not stated in the policy excerpts provided.

The policy excerpts include a broader billing-order rule that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the provider manual excerpt provided focuses on payment acceptance and recovery of deductible/coinsurance amounts rather than billing order [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources prohibit shifting Medicare-related cost-sharing to the dual-eligible member and require acceptance of the program payment as full payment: policy says providers "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], and the provider manual says payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reflect that Medicaid is secondary to Medicare for dual-eligible billing: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the provider manual limits total Medicare/Medicaid payment and addresses Medicare cost-sharing recovery [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is more specific about the type of cost-sharing at issue, stating that a pharmacy provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt speaks more generally about "any co-payment or co-insurance amount" for "Medicare Part C" benefit providers [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].

The provider manual includes a payment cap rule—"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "no additional payment will be made" if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]—which is not stated in the policy excerpts provided.

The policy excerpts include a broader billing-order rule that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the provider manual excerpt provided focuses on payment acceptance and recovery of deductible/coinsurance amounts rather than billing order [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt is the newest source overall and should guide current practice, but the provided provider manual text is truncated after "from Me..." so the full scope of the Part B recovery prohibition is not fully visible [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The July 2025 policy excerpt mentions that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" but does not provide a detailed co-payment rule in the excerpt, so it is background rather than a complete current definition [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — 2025-07-01].

• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-07-01 and the provider manual both require Medicare-related amounts to be accepted as payment in full, but the provider manual is more explicit that a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from the dual-eligible member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance states that for dual eligible enrollees, Medicaid is the payer of last resort and “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The newest policy excerpt also says that for Medicare Advantage claims, the provider must report the deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amount, and that “A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] The July 2025 policy update notes that duals are “subject to Medicare rules,” but does not add a more specific co-payment responsibility rule in the provided excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full” and that “a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] The provided manual excerpts also state that claims for dual-eligible members are tied to Medicare billing requirements and that Medicaid only pays in limited circumstances for certain excluded Part D drugs, but the excerpt does not provide a broader co-payment rule beyond the payment-in-full and no-recovery language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require Medicare-related amounts to be handled before Medicaid payment and reflect Medicare-first billing for dual-eligible members: Medicaid is “the payer of last resort” and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources prohibit providers from trying to recover certain cost-sharing amounts from dual-eligible individuals: policy says a Medicare Part C provider “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]; the manual says a Medicare Part B pharmacy provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more recent and more specific for current pharmacy guidance: it expressly says Medicare and Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full” and bars recovery of Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy excerpt only says duals are “subject to Medicare rules” without a specific co-payment responsibility rule in the provided July 2025 text [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are broader and not limited to pharmacy billing: they frame Medicaid as “the payer of last resort” and require third-party sources, including Medicare, to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], whereas the provider manual excerpt is narrower and pharmacy-specific, focusing on Medicare Part B payment-in-full and no recovery of deductible/coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require Medicare-related amounts to be handled before Medicaid payment and reflect Medicare-first billing for dual-eligible members: Medicaid is “the payer of last resort” and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources prohibit providers from trying to recover certain cost-sharing amounts from dual-eligible individuals: policy says a Medicare Part C provider “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]; the manual says a Medicare Part B pharmacy provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more recent and more specific for current pharmacy guidance: it expressly says Medicare and Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full” and bars recovery of Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy excerpt only says duals are “subject to Medicare rules” without a specific co-payment responsibility rule in the provided July 2025 text [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are broader and not limited to pharmacy billing: they frame Medicaid as “the payer of last resort” and require third-party sources, including Medicare, to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], whereas the provider manual excerpt is narrower and pharmacy-specific, focusing on Medicare Part B payment-in-full and no recovery of deductible/coinsurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define a general, all-setting co-payment responsibility rule for dual-eligible members from the policy excerpts alone; the clearest current guidance in the provided record is the newer provider manual pharmacy guidance dated 2025-10-01. The policy excerpts provided do not include a direct, comprehensive co-payment rule for all dual-eligible scenarios.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The policy source dated 2025-07-01 and the provider manual both require acceptance of Medicare/Medicaid payment as payment in full and bar direct recovery of co-payment/coinsurance from dual-eligible members, but the provider manual is more explicit about Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance limits.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible enrollees: \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted\" and \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. The newest policy chunk on co-payment responsibility states: \"A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare ...\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual evidence states: \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states: \"All claims for dual-eligible members su...\" and that Medicaid only pays in limited Part D-excluded circumstances [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources require payment acceptance as full payment rather than allowing extra collection from the dual-eligible member: \"The provider is required to accept the Medicare ...\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] and \"The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources prohibit direct recovery of member cost-sharing from dual-eligible individuals: \"cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] and \"cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific about the type of cost-sharing barred, expressly naming \"any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy chunk uses broader language about \"any co-payment or co-insurance amount\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].", "The policy source frames the issue as a billing hierarchy for dual eligibles, stating Medicaid is \"the payer of last resort\" and that Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], while the provider manual focuses more on payment limits and acceptance as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - The newest provider manual dated 2025-10-01 is more recent than the newest policy excerpt dated 2025-07-01, so it should guide current practice unless the user is asking about a historical period."}
test_id_15.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_15.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_15.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid pays only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, while the newest policy source dated 2025-02-01 addresses copayment override mechanics rather than defining Part D-excluded drug coverage. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence provided does not define how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs; instead, it says, "Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios: In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance)." [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says, "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01] It further states that "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy billing rules rather than general drug coverage, with the policy source describing a copayment override for certain scenarios and the provider manual describing when Medicaid may be billed for Medicare Part D members. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources are limited in scope to specific exceptions or special cases, not broad coverage of all drugs. The policy source limits its statement to a copayment override, and the provider manual limits payment to drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D and on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual gives direct coverage guidance for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, stating Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01] The policy source provided does not define that coverage rule and instead only describes a copayment override code. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01]

The provider manual is newer and therefore should guide current handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs by default, while the policy source is older and appears to be background on copayment processing rather than the substantive coverage rule. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01]
comparison: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy billing rules rather than general drug coverage, with the policy source describing a copayment override for certain scenarios and the provider manual describing when Medicaid may be billed for Medicare Part D members. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources are limited in scope to specific exceptions or special cases, not broad coverage of all drugs. The policy source limits its statement to a copayment override, and the provider manual limits payment to drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D and on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual gives direct coverage guidance for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, stating Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01] The policy source provided does not define that coverage rule and instead only describes a copayment override code. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01]

The provider manual is newer and therefore should guide current handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs by default, while the policy source is older and appears to be background on copayment processing rather than the substantive coverage rule. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a grounded definition of Medicare Part D-excluded drug handling, so the comparison is one-sided on that point. The provider manual excerpt is the controlling current guidance in the provided materials because it is newer and directly addresses the issue. [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios:
In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or
Coinsurance). Providers may also refer to the following resources:
• New York State Department of Health (NYS DOH) Office of Health Insurance Programs (OHIP)
Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication
ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy
/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20(D.0)
.pdf.
Questions:
• Billing questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343 9000.
• Questions regarding this guidance should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit at
‑
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
