policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_16.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_16.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_16.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for this issue. policy.text:
Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios:
In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or
Coinsurance). Providers may also refer to the following resources:
• New York State Department of Health (NYS DOH) Office of Health Insurance Programs (OHIP)
Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication
ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy
/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20(D.0)
.pdf.

Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_17.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_17.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_17.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_18.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid generally does not bill Medicare Part D members for prescription drugs except for drugs “specifically excluded by law” from Part D, while the newest policy source dated 2025-02-01 only addresses a copayment override and does not define Part D-excluded drug billing in the provided excerpt.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy excerpt only states: “Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios: In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value ‘04’ (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance).” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” It further says, “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy billing rules and use exception-based language rather than a blanket allowance. Policy: “Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Provider manual: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate that special handling applies in limited scenarios rather than ordinary billing. Policy: “Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Provider manual: “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives the substantive rule for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law” from Part D, with examples including “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide that definition. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt focuses on a copayment override mechanism, not on Part D exclusion criteria: “enter a value ‘04’ (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance).” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] The provider manual instead addresses whether Medicaid may be billed at all for Part D members and limits payment to legally excluded drug classes. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy billing rules and use exception-based language rather than a blanket allowance. Policy: “Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Provider manual: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate that special handling applies in limited scenarios rather than ordinary billing. Policy: “Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Provider manual: “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual gives the substantive rule for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law” from Part D, with examples including “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide that definition. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt focuses on a copayment override mechanism, not on Part D exclusion criteria: “enter a value ‘04’ (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance).” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] The provider manual instead addresses whether Medicaid may be billed at all for Part D members and limits payment to legally excluded drug classes. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpt is too limited to define how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs beyond a copayment override, so the provider manual should guide current billing interpretation for this issue. The policy source may still be relevant for the specific copayment override described, but it does not answer the Part D-excluded drug question in the supplied text.

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.

the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

test_id_18.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

test_id_19.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid pays only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the newest policy source dated 2025-02-01 does not specifically define Part D-excluded drugs and instead discusses practitioner-administered drugs and copayment overrides. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy chunk does not specifically define how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs; it instead states that pharmacies may use a copayment override for certain scenarios: "Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios: In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance)." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance says practitioner-administered drugs may be billed through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit and are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs, but it does not address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs specifically: "NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states: "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] It then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans," including "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie Medicaid pharmacy coverage to specific drug categories rather than a general open-ended rule: the policy source refers to practitioner-administered drugs on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs, and the provider manual refers to drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources use list-based limitations and do not suggest Medicaid pays for all drugs without restriction: the policy source says practitioner-administered drugs "are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs," while the provider manual says Medicaid "only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" when Part D excludes the drug class by law. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual directly addresses Medicare Part D-excluded drugs and says Medicaid pays only for those drugs when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy source provided does not specifically define Part D-excluded drugs; its newest guidance instead discusses a copayment override and older guidance on practitioner-administered drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019]

The provider manual gives examples of Part D-excluded drug classes, including "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs," while the policy excerpts do not provide any Part D-exclusion examples. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources tie Medicaid pharmacy coverage to specific drug categories rather than a general open-ended rule: the policy source refers to practitioner-administered drugs on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs, and the provider manual refers to drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources use list-based limitations and do not suggest Medicaid pays for all drugs without restriction: the policy source says practitioner-administered drugs "are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs," while the provider manual says Medicaid "only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" when Part D excludes the drug class by law. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual directly addresses Medicare Part D-excluded drugs and says Medicaid pays only for those drugs when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy source provided does not specifically define Part D-excluded drugs; its newest guidance instead discusses a copayment override and older guidance on practitioner-administered drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6,7 — Aug 01, 2019]

The provider manual gives examples of Part D-excluded drug classes, including "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs," while the policy excerpts do not provide any Part D-exclusion examples. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source. The policy excerpts do not expressly discuss Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, so the provider manual should guide current handling of that issue. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios:
In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or
Coinsurance). Providers may also refer to the following resources:
• New York State Department of Health (NYS DOH) Office of Health Insurance Programs (OHIP)
Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication
ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy
/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20(D.0)
.pdf.
Questions:
• Billing questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343 9000.
• Questions regarding this guidance should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit at
‑
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.

policy.text:
Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios:
In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or
Coinsurance). Providers may also refer to the following resources:
• New York State Department of Health (NYS DOH) Office of Health Insurance Programs (OHIP)
Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication

.pdf.
Questions:

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_20.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual specifically says Medicare must be billed first when services are covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable before billing Medicaid, because “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” and “all other available resources” must be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy updates say the same general rule, including that providers must bill “applicable third parties” before billing NYS Medicaid and that Medicaid is the “payor of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2, 3 — Apr 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source states that “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid” and that “[s]ervices covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” It also says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers are required to demonstrate participation in Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that a provider must pursue available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid and that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, “that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers are “required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid,” and the manual says available other coverage must be pursued before submitting a claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available payment sources: policy says “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort,” and the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific for Medicare: it says “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare,” while the policy source only states the broader rule to bill “applicable third parties” before Medicaid and does not single out Medicare in the cited April 2025 pharmacy reminder. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy source frames the rule generally as Medicaid being the “payer of last resort” for all liable third parties, while the provider manual adds operational Medicare enrollment language and a Medicare-first billing instruction for dual-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers are “required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid,” and the manual says available other coverage must be pursued before submitting a claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available payment sources: policy says “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort,” and the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific for Medicare: it says “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare,” while the policy source only states the broader rule to bill “applicable third parties” before Medicaid and does not single out Medicare in the cited April 2025 pharmacy reminder. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy source frames the rule generally as Medicaid being the “payer of last resort” for all liable third parties, while the provider manual adds operational Medicare enrollment language and a Medicare-first billing instruction for dual-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a pharmacy-specific Medicare-first instruction as explicit as the provider manual; they state only the general third-party-liability rule. The provider manual is newer and should guide current pharmacy billing practice for this issue unless you are reviewing a historical policy period. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Fee-For-Service Reminders from the Bureau of Dental Review
The Need to Bill Third-Party Insurers
Third-party insurers, including Medicare, provide reimbursement for various dental procedures. Since
Medicaid is the payer of last resort, the provider must bill the member’s third-party payers prior to requesting
payment from Medicaid. If the third party is a commercial plan, Medicaid will reimburse the difference only if
the total third-party payment(s) is (are) less than the lesser of the provider’s fee charged to the general public
or the fee developed by New York State Department of Health (DOH) for the specific procedure code. If the
third party is a Medicare Advantage plan, Medicaid will reimburse eighty-five percent (85%) of the patient
responsibility. Prior to initiating treatment which has been approved by a third-party insurance plan, the
provider should obtain a prior approval from Medicaid to ensure that the treatment plan falls within the current
guidelines of the Medicaid Program. Failure to do so may result in the denial of Medicaid benefits for these
services.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2020 Volume 36 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
