answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid requires providers to pursue available other coverage before Medicaid considers payment, and failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsible third party’s obligation to pay first. The newest policy source dated 2023-03-01 is older, so the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on crossover claims and prior approval after Medicaid payment. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — Mar 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that, for claims involving third-party coverage, providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting a claim to Medicaid, and any claim issues, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting the claim to Medicaid. The policy also says providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits from third-party payors to Medicaid claims. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment, and that Medicaid regulations at 18 NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. It further states that if a responsible third party should be paying as primary payor, that third party must pay first, and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome that responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: policy says providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before Medicaid, and the manual says all available resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place the third party’s payment/coverage responsibility ahead of Medicaid when another payer is available: policy says claim issues, including PA requirements, must be resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission, and the manual says the responsible third party must pay first. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is newer and adds an explicit rule that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsible third party’s obligation to pay first; the policy excerpts do not state that specific exception. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]

The policy excerpts specifically mention attaching an Explanation of Benefits from third-party payors to Medicaid claims, while the provider manual excerpt provided here does not mention EOB attachment. [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: policy says providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before Medicaid, and the manual says all available resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place the third party’s payment/coverage responsibility ahead of Medicaid when another payer is available: policy says claim issues, including PA requirements, must be resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission, and the manual says the responsible third party must pay first. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer and adds an explicit rule that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsible third party’s obligation to pay first; the policy excerpts do not state that specific exception. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]

The policy excerpts specifically mention attaching an Explanation of Benefits from third-party payors to Medicaid claims, while the provider manual excerpt provided here does not mention EOB attachment. [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept specifically as a crossover-claims rule after Medicaid payment; the excerpts address third-party liability, prior authorization, and billing order, but do not expressly define a post-Medicaid-payment crossover-claim prior approval process. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
12

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will require prior authorization (PA) for most medically necessary therapy visits,
unless the member receiving the service meets one or more of the criteria listed below:
• Children from birth to age 21 (until their 21st birthday)
• Members with developmental disabilities (members with restriction/exception code "95" on file)
• Members with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) [members with TBI* (use procedure code modifier ST on
claims) or restriction/exception code "81" on file]
• Members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid (dually eligible) when the service is covered by
Medicare
• Members receiving rehabilitation services as a hospital inpatient
• Members receiving rehabilitation services in a nursing home in which they reside
• Members receiving rehabilitation services provided by a certified home health agency (CHHA)
*TBI as defined in Public Health Law Article 27-cc: §2741.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, current guidance is that all available resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment, and a responsible third party must pay first; this source is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for this issue. The newest policy source dated 2022-04-01 is consistent that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid, but it does not specifically address prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid requires coordination of benefits and that providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid because Medicaid is the payor of last resort: "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]. Earlier policy guidance also says, "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided policy documents to define this concept for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says, "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states, "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. The manual also says, "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], but the provided excerpt is truncated and does not fully define crossover-claim prior approval after Medicaid payment. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided provider manual documents to define this concept for prior approval after Medicaid payment.
similarities: Both sources require third-party coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]; "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: "Medicaid is always the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]; "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more explicit about the rule's current framing, stating "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that the responsible third party "must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the same concept more generally as billing liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022].

Neither source excerpt specifically defines "prior approval after Medicaid payment" for crossover claims; the policy excerpt only says claim issues, including PA requirements, must be resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], and the provider manual excerpt is truncated after "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require third-party coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]; "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: "Medicaid is always the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]; "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more explicit about the rule's current framing, stating "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that the responsible third party "must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the same concept more generally as billing liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022].

Neither source excerpt specifically defines "prior approval after Medicaid payment" for crossover claims; the policy excerpt only says claim issues, including PA requirements, must be resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], and the provider manual excerpt is truncated after "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided excerpts do not contain a complete, grounded definition of "prior approval after Medicaid payment" for crossover claims. The provider manual is the more recent source overall (2025-10-01), so it should guide current interpretation, but the exact crossover-claim prior-approval rule is not fully stated in the supplied text.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
12

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will require prior authorization (PA) for most medically necessary therapy visits,
unless the member receiving the service meets one or more of the criteria listed below:
• Children from birth to age 21 (until their 21st birthday)
• Members with developmental disabilities (members with restriction/exception code "95" on file)
• Members with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) [members with TBI* (use procedure code modifier ST on
claims) or restriction/exception code "81" on file]
• Members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid (dually eligible) when the service is covered by
Medicare
• Members receiving rehabilitation services as a hospital inpatient
• Members receiving rehabilitation services in a nursing home in which they reside
• Members receiving rehabilitation services provided by a certified home health agency (CHHA)
*TBI as defined in Public Health Law Article 27-cc: §2741.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

test_id_28.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_28.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_28.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_29.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_29.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. For crossover claims, the current manual says Medicaid is secondary to other available coverage and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to bill the primary payer first; the policy excerpts are older, with the newest policy source dated 2023-03-01. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,17-18 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and providers must bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting to Medicaid: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. They also state that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" and that "Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. The newest policy excerpt provided is about DMEPOS transition and says some items "require pre-approval" and that authorization requirements were "temporarily relaxed" during the transition period starting April 1, 2023, but it does not specifically define crossover-claim prior approval after Medicaid payment [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual says Medicaid must be used only after other available resources: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. It further states that if another party is the primary payor, "that responsible third party must pay first" and that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also notes Medicare Part B coverage for certain drugs and related covered items, but the provided excerpt does not specifically define crossover-claim prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17,18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed or used before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: "To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy excerpt provided is dated 2023-03-01 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-18 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01].

The policy excerpts explicitly say third-party claim issues, including "prior authorization (PA) requirements," must be resolved before Medicaid submission, while the provider manual excerpt instead says "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." and focuses on pursuing other coverage first; the provided manual excerpt does not clearly state the same crossover-claim PA rule after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be billed or used before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: "To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy excerpt provided is dated 2023-03-01 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-18 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01].

The policy excerpts explicitly say third-party claim issues, including "prior authorization (PA) requirements," must be resolved before Medicaid submission, while the provider manual excerpt instead says "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." and focuses on pursuing other coverage first; the provided manual excerpt does not clearly state the same crossover-claim PA rule after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for crossover claims specifically as a post-Medicaid-payment prior approval requirement. The excerpts support a general prior-billing/prior-authorization-before-Medicaid rule, but they do not clearly state a distinct crossover-claim prior approval process after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims

implementation of this initiative.

NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.

at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.

(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

Code

PA Required Call Prime

Government Solutions. This is

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

test_id_29.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. For crossover claims, the current manual says Medicaid is secondary to other available coverage and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to bill the primary payer first; the policy excerpts are older, with the newest policy source dated 2023-03-01. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,17-18 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01]", "policy_definition": "Policy documents state that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and providers must bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting to Medicaid: \"providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. They also state that \"Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid\" and that \"Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. The newest policy excerpt provided is about DMEPOS transition and says some items \"require pre-approval\" and that authorization requirements were \"temporarily relaxed\" during the transition period starting April 1, 2023, but it does not specifically define crossover-claim prior approval after Medicaid payment [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The provider manual says Medicaid must be used only after other available resources: \"Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment\" and \"a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. It further states that if another party is the primary payor, \"that responsible third party must pay first\" and that \"Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also notes Medicare Part B coverage for certain drugs and related covered items, but the provided excerpt does not specifically define crossover-claim prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17,18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources require other coverage to be billed or used before Medicaid payment: \"providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and \"a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: \"To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and \"all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy excerpt provided is dated 2023-03-01 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-18 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — 2023-03-01].", "The policy excerpts explicitly say third-party claim issues, including \"prior authorization (PA) requirements,\" must be resolved before Medicaid submission, while the provider manual excerpt instead says \"Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...\" and focuses on pursuing other coverage first; the provided manual excerpt does not clearly state the same crossover-claim PA rule after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for crossover claims specifically as a post-Medicaid-payment prior approval requirement. The excerpts support a general prior-billing/prior-authorization-before-Medicaid rule, but they do not clearly state a distinct crossover-claim prior approval process after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."}
test_id_29.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_29.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_29.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_30.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_30.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid requires providers to pursue other coverage first, and failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome that responsibility; this source is more up to date overall and should guide current handling of crossover claims. The newest policy source dated 2023-03-01 is older and mainly says third-party authorization issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission, so it is background unless you need historical context.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that for crossover/third-party claims, providers must resolve claim issues with the third party, including prior authorization requirements, before submitting the claim to Medicaid: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] The newer policy material also reinforces that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and that providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting to Medicaid: "To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual says Medicaid requires use of all available resources before payment and that a provider must pursue other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that if there is a responsible third party, that party must pay first, and that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require third-party coverage to be addressed before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other payors: "NYS Medicaid is the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts expressly say third-party claim issues, including PA requirements, must be resolved before Medicaid submission: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual excerpt does not expressly say the third party's PA requirements must be resolved first; it instead says "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: "Effective Date: 2025-10-01" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy source provided is older: "Effective Date: 2023-03-01" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — Mar 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources require third-party coverage to be addressed before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other payors: "NYS Medicaid is the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts expressly say third-party claim issues, including PA requirements, must be resolved before Medicaid submission: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual excerpt does not expressly say the third party's PA requirements must be resolved first; it instead says "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: "Effective Date: 2025-10-01" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy source provided is older: "Effective Date: 2023-03-01" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:6 — Mar 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provider manual citation is truncated in the provided chunk, so the exact full sentence after "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit ..." is not fully visible; the comparison therefore relies only on the grounded text shown. Also, the policy excerpts are older than the provider manual, so the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
12

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will require prior authorization (PA) for most medically necessary therapy visits,
unless the member receiving the service meets one or more of the criteria listed below:
• Children from birth to age 21 (until their 21st birthday)
• Members with developmental disabilities (members with restriction/exception code "95" on file)
• Members with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) [members with TBI* (use procedure code modifier ST on
claims) or restriction/exception code "81" on file]
• Members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid (dually eligible) when the service is covered by
Medicare
• Members receiving rehabilitation services as a hospital inpatient
• Members receiving rehabilitation services in a nursing home in which they reside
• Members receiving rehabilitation services provided by a certified home health agency (CHHA)
*TBI as defined in Public Health Law Article 27-cc: §2741.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the benefit transition, that require pre-approval. To ensure
a smooth transition for MMC enrollees, DMEPOS authorization requirements will be temporarily
relaxed (e.g., claims will not deny) during the transition period which starts April 1, 2023.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or

test_id_31.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-04-01 and 2022-10-01 say COB requires billing liable third parties first because NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, but the provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation rule for work-related injuries. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents define COB as requiring providers to bill applicable liable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] The October 2022 update states the same core rule: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual’s newest COB guidance is more specific: “If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states an exception for unrelated drugs: “If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’ in field 309-C9.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid/NYRx is billed: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort rather than first payer: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy documents state the general COB rule for all applicable third parties and available resources: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025], while the provider manual gives a specific Workers’ Compensation rule for work-related injuries [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual includes an explicit exception not stated in the policy excerpts: “If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case… NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid/NYRx is billed: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort rather than first payer: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents state the general COB rule for all applicable third parties and available resources: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025], while the provider manual gives a specific Workers’ Compensation rule for work-related injuries [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual includes an explicit exception not stated in the policy excerpts: “If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case… NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source beyond the general last-resort/third-party-first rule; the policy excerpts do not provide a detailed operational COB billing procedure. The provider manual is newer overall and should guide current pharmacy COB handling, but the policy excerpts remain useful background for the general Medicaid last-resort rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
