This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_48.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, and this source should receive more weight by default because it is newer than the newest policy source dated 2022-07-01. The policy source is consistent in requiring 340B drugs to be identified on the claim, but it does not state the reimbursement amount as clearly as the provider manual does.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says 340B drugs must be identified on the claim: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy also says 340B claims are "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider" and must be "properly identified as 340B" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at "340B Acquisition Cost": "340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also requires claim-level identification and cost reporting for Medicaid 340B drug claims, including "420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20" and "409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require 340B drugs to be identified on the claim: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] and "The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to 340B acquisition cost rather than a standard drug price: "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019] and "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is explicit that reimbursement is at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy excerpt focuses on claim identification and says only that 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].

The provider manual includes specific NCPDP field instructions such as "409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not include comparable field-level reimbursement instructions [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].
comparison: Both sources require 340B drugs to be identified on the claim: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] and "The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to 340B acquisition cost rather than a standard drug price: "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019] and "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is explicit that reimbursement is at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy excerpt focuses on claim identification and says only that 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].

The provider manual includes specific NCPDP field instructions such as "409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not include comparable field-level reimbursement instructions [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a fully explicit reimbursement rule for 340B drugs in the newest policy chunk; the clearest reimbursement language appears in the older 2019 policy excerpt and the newer 2025 provider manual. The provider manual is the more up-to-date source for this issue and should guide current reimbursement handling unless a historical policy question is intended.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.

website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_49.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

test_id_50.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost for 340B purchased drugs, and this source should receive more weight by default because it is newer than the newest policy source dated 2024-02-01. The policy sources are consistent with 340B claims needing correct identification and ingredient-cost controls, but the provider manual is the more up-to-date guidance for current reimbursement methodology.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that 340B drugs must be identified on claims and that reimbursement depends on correct claim-level identification and ingredient cost: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] They also state that 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP D.0 format are "required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care (MMC) members" and "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019]. The newest policy source adds that "A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied" and "Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that the reimbursement methodology for 340B drugs is "340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost" and that the pharmacy is reimbursed at "340B Acquisition Cost" for 340B purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also explains that, for 340B pharmacy drug claims, covered entities must follow federal duplicate-discount rules and HRSA requirements when using 340B drugs for Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require 340B drugs to be identified correctly on the claim before payment: the policy says they "must be identified by appending the UD modifier" and "properly identified as 340B" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019], and the provider manual addresses "340B Pharmacy Drug Claims" under 340B program rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie reimbursement to the 340B acquisition cost rather than an unqualified standard price: policy says claims are "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost" and claims above the ceiling price are denied [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024], while the provider manual says 340B purchased drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The newest provider manual gives the current reimbursement methodology directly: "340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost" / "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts describe identification and denial/resubmission rules and do not state the reimbursement table as directly in the newest policy chunk [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].

Policy adds a specific claim-processing rule not stated in the provider manual excerpt: "A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied" and "Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]. The provider manual excerpt instead emphasizes reimbursement methodology and federal duplicate-discount/HRSA requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require 340B drugs to be identified correctly on the claim before payment: the policy says they "must be identified by appending the UD modifier" and "properly identified as 340B" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019], and the provider manual addresses "340B Pharmacy Drug Claims" under 340B program rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie reimbursement to the 340B acquisition cost rather than an unqualified standard price: policy says claims are "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost" and claims above the ceiling price are denied [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024], while the provider manual says 340B purchased drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].

The newest provider manual gives the current reimbursement methodology directly: "340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost" / "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts describe identification and denial/resubmission rules and do not state the reimbursement table as directly in the newest policy chunk [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].

Policy adds a specific claim-processing rule not stated in the provider manual excerpt: "A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied" and "Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]. The provider manual excerpt instead emphasizes reimbursement methodology and federal duplicate-discount/HRSA requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided provider manual excerpt is newer and more directly states the reimbursement amount, so it should guide current reimbursement questions by default. The policy excerpts are still relevant for claim identification and denial rules, but the provided chunks do not include a full standalone policy reimbursement table for 340B drugs.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)

409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using

in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. It says home infusion drugs are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, while the newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses coverage for practitioner-administered drugs but does not specifically define home infusion billing responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:6 — Jul 01, 2022]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in the listed compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy also states billing responsibility is on the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond practitioner-administered drug coverage and pharmacy billing responsibility.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring practitioner administration to be available through both benefits: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie coverage to drugs administered by a health care provider/practitioner rather than ordinary retail dispensing: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] and “including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate billing/coverage depends on the correct benefit or payer pathway rather than a single universal payment method: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment: “does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically address home infusion bundled payment.

The policy excerpts place billing responsibility on the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual excerpt says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” without assigning billing responsibility to a specific party [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources tie coverage to drugs administered by a health care provider/practitioner rather than ordinary retail dispensing: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] and “including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate billing/coverage depends on the correct benefit or payer pathway rather than a single universal payment method: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment: “does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically address home infusion bundled payment.

The policy excerpts place billing responsibility on the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual excerpt says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” without assigning billing responsibility to a specific party [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct home infusion-specific coverage rule, so the policy-side answer is limited to practitioner-administered drug coverage and pharmacy billing responsibility. The provider manual is newer and more specific for current home infusion guidance, so it should guide the user now unless the question is historical.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses practitioner-administered drugs generally and billing, but it does not specifically define home infusion coverage in the provided excerpts.

similarities: Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than describing automatic bundled payment coverage: the policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources recognize drugs administered by a health care professional as covered only within defined billing/coverage frameworks: the policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods for FDA-approved or supported medically accepted indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the manual says home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider may be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically define home infusion coverage; they only address practitioner-administered drugs generally [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

The provider manual gives the current billing framework for home infusion drugs and supplies as either a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpts only assign billing responsibility generally to the pharmacy to bill “timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].
comparison: Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than describing automatic bundled payment coverage: the policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources recognize drugs administered by a health care professional as covered only within defined billing/coverage frameworks: the policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods for FDA-approved or supported medically accepted indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the manual says home infusion drugs requiring administration by a health care provider may be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual specifically addresses home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically define home infusion coverage; they only address practitioner-administered drugs generally [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a home-infusion-specific coverage rule, so the policy side is incomplete for this exact concept. For current guidance on home infusion drugs and billing, the 2025 provider manual is the more recent and more directly applicable source.

test_id_53.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The manual says home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, while the newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses coverage for practitioner-administered drugs but does not specifically define home infusion billing responsibility.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the listed compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define home infusion drug coverage or billing responsibility for the policy source.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring practitioner administration to be available “by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources address drugs administered by a practitioner route and tie coverage to Medicaid-covered use rather than a blanket home-infusion-specific payment rule: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] and “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place billing/coverage within Medicaid benefit structure rather than a separate home-infusion bundle: the policy source covers certain administered drugs under NYS Medicaid FFS [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion and that home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit: “does not provide a bundled payment... must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] The policy source does not provide that home-infusion-specific billing rule.

The policy source’s billing responsibility language is pharmacy-focused and general: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] The provider manual excerpt provided does not assign billing responsibility to the pharmacy in those terms, instead stating the drugs/supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources address drugs administered by a practitioner route and tie coverage to Medicaid-covered use rather than a blanket home-infusion-specific payment rule: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] and “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place billing/coverage within Medicaid benefit structure rather than a separate home-infusion bundle: the policy source covers certain administered drugs under NYS Medicaid FFS [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion and that home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit: “does not provide a bundled payment... must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] The policy source does not provide that home-infusion-specific billing rule.

The policy source’s billing responsibility language is pharmacy-focused and general: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] The provider manual excerpt provided does not assign billing responsibility to the pharmacy in those terms, instead stating the drugs/supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a home-infusion-specific coverage rule, so the policy-side answer is incomplete for this exact concept. The provider manual is the more recent and more directly applicable source for home infusion coverage and billing, but the excerpts do not identify a single responsible billing entity beyond requiring billing through the pharmacy or medical benefit.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

[New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.

[Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than on the member: the policy says “it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources reflect that these drugs are covered through Medicaid billing pathways tied to specific benefit/payment rules rather than as an unrestricted payment arrangement: the policy covers certain administered drugs under defined indication criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies are billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit and not under a bundled payment [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual specifically addresses home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments,” and that they “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically define home infusion coverage or a bundled-payment rule. policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.
