test_id_55.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_55.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs are not covered through a bundled payment; they must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, so the provider manual should guide current billing/coverage questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 is older and says NYRx will pay for covered products and services when the prescriber is enrolled, but it does not give the same home-infusion-specific billing rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYRx covers covered products and services when the prescriber is enrolled: "NYRx will pay for covered products and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023] Older policy guidance also states that certain practitioner-administered drugs are available through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit and "may be billed directly to the Me..." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond those general practitioner-administered-drug billing rules. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source states: "Home Infusion The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It further says, "Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says home infusion drugs are among drugs requiring administration by a health care provider that are available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources recognize that drugs requiring administration by a practitioner/provider can be available through both the medical/pharmacy benefit structure: "available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025] and "available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019].

Both sources tie payment to billing through the appropriate Medicaid pathway rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: "Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] and "may be billed directly to the Me..." for practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019].
differences: The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion: "does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not state that home infusion is non-bundled.

The policy excerpts focus on prescriber enrollment and pharmacy payment responsibility: "NYRx will pay for covered products and services... when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023] and "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual specifically says home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources recognize that drugs requiring administration by a practitioner/provider can be available through both the medical/pharmacy benefit structure: "available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025] and "available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019].

Both sources tie payment to billing through the appropriate Medicaid pathway rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: "Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] and "may be billed directly to the Me..." for practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019].

The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion: "does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not state that home infusion is non-bundled.

The policy excerpts focus on prescriber enrollment and pharmacy payment responsibility: "NYRx will pay for covered products and services... when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023] and "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual specifically says home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the more recent source overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy excerpts provided are older and do not contain a home-infusion-specific coverage rule; the strongest policy billing language is general pharmacy billing responsibility, not a direct home-infusion instruction. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prescribers and Enrollment
Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be
enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an “Individual Billing” provider or as an “Ordering, Prescribing, Referring,
or Attending” (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products
and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx. There are two paths
of enrollment, as a biller or OPRA privder:
Physicians, Nurse Practitioners (NPs), Physician Assistants (PAs), Podiatrists, Dentists,
Optometrists, Audiologists, and Certified Nurse Midwives Providers who wish to:
1. Enroll and receive payment for covered NYS Medicaid services should apply as an “Individual Billing
Medicaid”. Information regarding how to enroll is available on the eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and
Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/; or
2. Enroll as an OPRA provider (non-billing) should alternatively enroll as an OPRA provider on the
eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/
info/ProviderEnrollment/.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.

The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.

administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.

test_id_56.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, while the provider manual excerpt does not directly define naloxone reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when it is “prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member” or “dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024] When dispensing naloxone OTC via a standing order, the policy requires that the member specifically requested the item on the date of service or that there was a pharmacist-initiated dispensing based on a determination of need/risk on the date of service, and that the pharmacy submits “one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024] Older policy language also states, “Reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual. The newest provider manual excerpt only states that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology,” but it is in the context of billing both a drug and administration and does not mention naloxone specifically. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources address reimbursement/payment through pharmacy billing rules rather than a separate naloxone-specific payment rule: the policy says naloxone OTC must be dispensed under specified conditions, including standing order compliance, and the manual says the medication is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie reimbursement to compliance with applicable billing/claim requirements: the policy requires dispensing “according to: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines; NYS laws, rules, and regulations,” and the manual says claims must be submitted separately and billed using the ordering prescriber’s NPI and NDC for the drug. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy gives naloxone-specific reimbursement conditions, stating NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, including a one-course-of-therapy/no-refills limit. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not provide naloxone-specific reimbursement criteria. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual excerpt is broader and billing-focused, stating generally that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology,” while the policy excerpt is narrower and specifically addresses OTC naloxone under Medicaid coverage rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024]
comparison: Both sources address reimbursement/payment through pharmacy billing rules rather than a separate naloxone-specific payment rule: the policy says naloxone OTC must be dispensed under specified conditions, including standing order compliance, and the manual says the medication is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie reimbursement to compliance with applicable billing/claim requirements: the policy requires dispensing “according to: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines; NYS laws, rules, and regulations,” and the manual says claims must be submitted separately and billed using the ordering prescriber’s NPI and NDC for the drug. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy gives naloxone-specific reimbursement conditions, stating NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, including a one-course-of-therapy/no-refills limit. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not provide naloxone-specific reimbursement criteria. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual excerpt is broader and billing-focused, stating generally that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology,” while the policy excerpt is narrower and specifically addresses OTC naloxone under Medicaid coverage rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024]

The 2019 policy language is older background only and should not override the 2024 policy or the 2025 provider manual billing language absent a direct naloxone-specific manual citation. policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

member; and

• the drug item(s) are dispensed according to:
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;

NYS Medicaid Policy.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.

provider_manual.text:
eMedNY Edit Number/Message NCPDP Response Code/Description
"02326" – Drug only covered in "8H" – Product/service only covered on
compound compound claim
Non-Reimbursable Ingredients
Payment will only be issued for drugs found on the eMedNY "Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs" found
here: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. If an ineligible drug or drug product is included in the compound,
the claim will deny. The pharmacy provider may then elect to receive payment only for those reimbursable drugs by
resubmitting the claim with of "08−Process Compound for Approved Ingredients" in field 420-DK (Submission
Clarification Code) when all other coverage criteria is met.
Please note: Submission Clarification Code of "08" should only be utilized in accordance with NYS Medicaid policy. The
NYS Department of Health (DOH) will monitor the use of these codes.

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_56.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_56.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_57.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed by a NYS Medicaid practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, while the provider manual excerpt does not specifically define naloxone reimbursement.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when it is “prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy also stated that “Reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual. The newest provider manual excerpt only states that “The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” and that “Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources indicate reimbursement is tied to Medicaid pharmacy billing/reimbursement rules rather than a general cash-payment concept: “Reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019] and “The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources support that reimbursement depends on proper Medicaid-related conditions being met, including dispensing/billing requirements: “dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] and “Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source specifically addresses naloxone and says OTC naloxone is covered when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order: “NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not specifically mention naloxone reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual excerpt is broader and more general, describing reimbursement for “prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” and billing by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy with the member CIN [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy source gives naloxone-specific OTC coverage conditions and does not state the broader list-based rule in the provided excerpt [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024].
comparison: Both sources indicate reimbursement is tied to Medicaid pharmacy billing/reimbursement rules rather than a general cash-payment concept: “Reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019] and “The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources support that reimbursement depends on proper Medicaid-related conditions being met, including dispensing/billing requirements: “dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] and “Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source specifically addresses naloxone and says OTC naloxone is covered when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order: “NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not specifically mention naloxone reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual excerpt is broader and more general, describing reimbursement for “prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” and billing by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy with the member CIN [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy source gives naloxone-specific OTC coverage conditions and does not state the broader list-based rule in the provided excerpt [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpts provided do not contain a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule, so the current naloxone answer is grounded primarily in the 2024 policy update. The 2025 provider manual is newer overall, but the provided excerpt is too general to replace the naloxone-specific policy language.

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs, which can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
• Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual
Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).
Smoking Cessation Policy
• Smoking cessation therapy consists of certain prescription and non-prescription agents.
• Some smoking cessation therapies may be used together. Professional judgment should be exercised when
dispensing multiple smoking cessation products.
• For all smoking cessation products, the member must have a prescription or fiscal order.
• NY Medicaid reimburses for over-the-counter nicotine patches included on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
Emergency Contraception Drug Policy
Both prescription and OTC Emergency Contraception are a covered benefit for all Medicaid members without age
restriction. This includes individuals enrolled in the Family Planning Benefit Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_57.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

test_id_57.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_58.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 still shows naloxone reimbursement through pharmacy billing methodology, but the provider manual gives the current reimbursement/billing framework for drug and administration claims. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents show that NYS Medicaid covers naloxone OTC when it is “prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024]. The April 2024 update adds that “For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2 — Apr 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that “For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that “For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field” and that the pharmacy “will be reimbursed” for the administration when billed that way [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources say the drug is reimbursed when billed using NDC and a prescriber NPI in the Prescriber ID field: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2 — Apr 01, 2024] and the same wording appears in the provider manual [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie naloxone coverage/reimbursement to pharmacy dispensing under Medicaid rather than describing a separate non-pharmacy payment pathway: “NYS Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024], and the provider manual addresses pharmacy claim submission and reimbursement for the drug and administration [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy documents explicitly describe OTC naloxone coverage conditions—“prescribed by the practitioner ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024]—while the provider manual excerpt does not restate those OTC eligibility conditions [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds administration billing guidance that is not shown in the policy excerpts: “For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources say the drug is reimbursed when billed using NDC and a prescriber NPI in the Prescriber ID field: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2 — Apr 01, 2024] and the same wording appears in the provider manual [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie naloxone coverage/reimbursement to pharmacy dispensing under Medicaid rather than describing a separate non-pharmacy payment pathway: “NYS Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024], and the provider manual addresses pharmacy claim submission and reimbursement for the drug and administration [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents explicitly describe OTC naloxone coverage conditions—“prescribed by the practitioner ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024]—while the provider manual excerpt does not restate those OTC eligibility conditions [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds administration billing guidance that is not shown in the policy excerpts: “For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define whether naloxone reimbursement differs by setting, member category, or product form beyond the OTC/standing-order and NDC/NPI billing language quoted here. The December 2022 chunk is background only and does not add reimbursement rules [mu_no14_dec22_pr.pdf::0000 — Dec 01, 2022].

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

December 2022
Volume 38 | Number 14
Opioid Overdose Prevention
The New York State (NYS) Department of Health (DOH) has recently released two brochures on Naloxone,
the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose, for individuals who are pregnant or who’ve
recently given birth, as well as their support person(s):
• Patient education brochure tilted Do You or Someone You Know Take Opioids?

kept on file at the pharmacy.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical Exception Request
If a prescriber has determined that a drug, not on the NY Medicaid List of Reimbursable Drugs is the only treatment
appropriate for a member, the prescriber may submit a letter of medical necessity and supporting documentation to
NYRx by fax at 518-473-5508 for a review of available coverage
4.0 Basis of Payment
Covered Outpatient Drugs (COD) are defined in federal statute.
Prescription Drugs
Pharmacy reimbursement for prescription drugs under the New York State Medicaid Program is established in law.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).

test_id_59.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, while the provider manual adds billing/reimbursement mechanics for claims submitted via NDC and reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) “when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] For dispensing naloxone OTC via a standing order, the policy requires that the member specifically requested the item on the date of service or the pharmacist initiated dispensing based on a determination of need/risk on the date of service, that the pharmacy submit “one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills,” and that the drug be dispensed according to FDA guidelines and NYS laws, rules, and regulations. [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:8 — Sep 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024] The older 2019 update adds only that reimbursement is made “in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says that if billing for both the drug and administration, “these claims must be submitted separately,” with the drug claim using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field and the administration claim using procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual beyond these billing/reimbursement instructions. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie reimbursement/coverage to compliant billing and dispensing conditions rather than an unrestricted payment rule: the policy requires dispensing under the standing order and according to FDA/NYS rules, while the manual requires NDC billing and reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate naloxone is reimbursable/covered in the Medicaid pharmacy context when the applicable requirements are met. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source defines when OTC naloxone is covered: it must be prescribed by the practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, with standing-order conditions including member request or pharmacist-initiated need/risk determination, one course of therapy (two doses), and no refills. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:8 — Sep 01, 2023] The provider manual excerpt does not define those coverage conditions. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds reimbursement mechanics not stated in the policy excerpt: billing via NDC, use of the ordering prescriber’s NPI for the drug claim, separate submission for drug and administration, and procedure code 96372 for administration. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt instead references general Medicaid supervision and billing rules without those specific claim instructions. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019]
comparison: Both sources tie reimbursement/coverage to compliant billing and dispensing conditions rather than an unrestricted payment rule: the policy requires dispensing under the standing order and according to FDA/NYS rules, while the manual requires NDC billing and reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate naloxone is reimbursable/covered in the Medicaid pharmacy context when the applicable requirements are met. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source defines when OTC naloxone is covered: it must be prescribed by the practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, with standing-order conditions including member request or pharmacist-initiated need/risk determination, one course of therapy (two doses), and no refills. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:8 — Sep 01, 2023] The provider manual excerpt does not define those coverage conditions. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds reimbursement mechanics not stated in the policy excerpt: billing via NDC, use of the ordering prescriber’s NPI for the drug claim, separate submission for drug and administration, and procedure code 96372 for administration. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt instead references general Medicaid supervision and billing rules without those specific claim instructions. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — Feb 01, 2019]
answers.caveats: The provided provider manual excerpt is a billing-guideline snippet and does not include a naloxone-specific coverage definition; the policy excerpts are naloxone-specific but do not provide the detailed claim-format instructions found in the manual. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]

policy.text:
When dispensing naloxone OTC
via a standing order, all the following must apply:
• a NYS Medicaid member had specifically requested the item on the date of service (DOS); or
• a pharmacist-initiated dispensing per a determination of need/risk on DOS for a NYS Medicaid
member; and
• a pharmacy submits one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills; and
• the drug item(s) are dispensed according to:
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;
NYS laws, rules, and regulations; and
NYS Medicaid Policy.
Billing a Standing Order:
1. Enter a value of "5" in the Prescription Origin Code field 419-DJ to indicate pharmacy dispensing;

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.

test_id_59.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_59.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under the state standing order, while the provider manual excerpt does not provide a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when it is “prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member” or “dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024] When dispensing naloxone OTC via a standing order, the pharmacy must have a member request on the date of service or a pharmacist-initiated dispensing based on a determination of need/risk, and “a pharmacy submits one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual. The newest provider manual excerpt only states generally that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology,” but it does not mention naloxone specifically. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources support reimbursement/coverage through pharmacy billing rather than cash-only treatment: the policy source says naloxone is covered when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order, and the provider manual says the pharmacy is reimbursed for medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources are pharmacy-focused and tie payment to claim/billing conditions rather than a general medical benefit statement. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source is naloxone-specific and OTC-specific: it says NYS Medicaid covers “naloxone over the counter (OTC)” when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not mention naloxone or OTC naloxone specifically. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source adds naloxone-specific dispensing limits and conditions, including “one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills” and request/need-risk requirements, while the provider manual excerpt provides no comparable naloxone-specific limits. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources support reimbursement/coverage through pharmacy billing rather than cash-only treatment: the policy source says naloxone is covered when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order, and the provider manual says the pharmacy is reimbursed for medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources are pharmacy-focused and tie payment to claim/billing conditions rather than a general medical benefit statement. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source is naloxone-specific and OTC-specific: it says NYS Medicaid covers “naloxone over the counter (OTC)” when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not mention naloxone or OTC naloxone specifically. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source adds naloxone-specific dispensing limits and conditions, including “one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills” and request/need-risk requirements, while the provider manual excerpt provides no comparable naloxone-specific limits. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — Feb 01, 2024]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpts provided do not contain a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule, so the current reimbursement answer is grounded primarily in the February 2024 Medicaid Update. The provider manual is newer overall (2025-10-01), but the cited excerpt is too general to override the naloxone-specific policy language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]

September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
